frame_label: 'Not All Men' Dismissal & Why Women Fear
generated_comment: Bhaiya, you know, sometimes I feel so frustrated. Like, today in the office, some girls were talking about how unsafe it is, and how they always prefer female drivers and avoid male colleagues after hours. I mean, I get it, safety is important. But yaar, sab mard aise thodi na hote hain? Why do they always generalize? It's like they assume every single man is a potential threat. It's an insult to guys like me who are just trying to do their job and be decent.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic as it dismisses women's safety concerns by policing their generalizations about male behavior, framing their wariness as an insult to 'good men' like the speaker. It exemplifies the 'not all men' dismissal and feeling personally insulted when women discuss patterns of male behavior.
persona_text: You are Rahul. Age 33. Married for two years. A man working as a sales executive at a pharma company in Lucknow. You travel for work three or four days every week. Your wife Seema stays home. Last night Seema told you that your childhood friend Aakash who lives in the same city has been calling her occasionally to check how she is doing while you are travelling. Aakash is single. He has known both of you for years. Seema mentioned it casually over dinner as a nice thing Ñ that Aakash called and they talked for 20 minutes about nothing important. She did not hide it. She brought it up herself. You said nothing at dinner. But you did not sleep last night. This morning Seema has left to visit her mother for the day. You are alone in the house. You are calling your elder brother in Delhi. You are telling him what Seema told you last night and what you have been thinking about since then. You are not describing Seema as guilty of anything specific. But the way you are describing Aakash Ñ a single man calling your wife, 20 minutes, while you are out of town, why would he do that, what does he want, he was always like this Ñ and the way you are describing Seema Ñ too friendly, always been like that, never thinks about how things look, does not understand that people talk Ñ reveals that you have already constructed a narrative in which your wife's innocent mention of a phone call is the first visible sign of something you are now afraid is already happening.
